title: The Cyber Imperative
subtitle: The walls are aging from within while the tide rises from without. AI-driven modernization is no longer separable from security.
abstract: Alberta runs one of the strongest cyber security programs in Canada, and over the last 18 months it has held the line against a steady run of attacks. Holding it is getting harder for two reasons at once. The tide outside the walls is rising: cyber controls now block 189 million connection attempts a day, more than double two years ago, and AI moves an exploit from proof of concept to live in hours, often ahead of the vendor patch. The walls themselves are aging: much of the estate runs on technology a decade or more past vendor support, where no patch exists to apply. The software supply chain has become a primary target, and a single incident carries a heavy and recurring cost. This paper makes the case that AI-driven modernization is a security requirement.

hero_image.alt: Old stone fortress walls part-submerged by a fast-rising sea, defenders still holding the upper ramparts.
hero_image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of old stone fortress walls standing against a fast-rising sea, water already lapping over the lower ramparts while defenders hold the line above. A sense of strong defenses meeting a tide that keeps climbing. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

slides.title: The Cyber Imperative
slides.caption: Paper 2 · Cybersecurity
slides.subcaption: The walls age from within while the tide rises from without.
slides.text: The Cyber Imperative. Alberta runs one of the strongest cyber programs in Canada, and for eighteen months it has held the line. Holding it is getting harder for two reasons at once. The tide outside the walls is rising, and the walls themselves are aging. This paper makes the case that modernization and security are now the same case.

slides.title: 189 million a day
slides.caption: The tide, rising
slides.subcaption: Up from 126.3 million the year before, and 81.2 million the year before that.
slides.text: The tide. In 2024-25, Alberta's cyber controls blocked an average of one hundred and eighty-nine million attempts to connect to the government network every day. The year before it was one hundred and twenty-six million, and the year before that eighty-one million. The province attributes the surge to sophisticated nation-state actors and financially motivated criminal groups.

slides.title: What changed: speed
slides.caption: Discovery is no longer the slow part

left.label: Before
left.body: Finding a flaw was the slow part. An exploit took weeks to build.

right.label: Now
right.body: Proof of concept to live in hours, often ahead of the vendor patch. AI surfaces flaws buried for years.

slides.text: What changed most is speed. We have weathered AI-orchestrated attacks where an exploit moves from proof of concept to live in hours, often before the vendor's patch arrives. The same models now find flaws that sat undocumented for years. Project Glasswing surfaced more than ten thousand vulnerabilities in its first month, and Mozilla fixed two hundred and seventy-one in Firefox using the Claude Mythos model. The capability sits on both sides of the wall.

slides.title: You cannot patch what the vendor no longer ships

visual_config.text: You cannot patch what the vendor no longer ships.

slides.text: Now the walls. We maintain roughly one thousand two hundred and eighty applications, many of them aging. A meaningful share runs on technology more than a decade past vendor support, where for some there is no patch left to apply. You cannot patch what the vendor no longer ships. This is why patching alone cannot answer the problem, and why the cyber case and the modernization case are one case.

slides.title: A sprawling attack surface
slides.caption: Wide, and widening

items.label: Too many stacks
items.desc: More operating systems, languages, and dependencies than anyone can patch.

items.label: Committed secrets
items.desc: Keys and tokens in source, found by AI in 2025 and cleared.

items.label: Supply chain
items.desc: Megalodon hit 5,500+ repositories; a hijacked Axios reached 100M downloads a week.

items.label: Foreign contributions
items.desc: Traced through commit histories and moved off.

slides.text: The surface is wide. Too many operating systems, languages, and dependencies. Secrets committed into source, found by AI and cleared. Contributions from foreign countries, traced and moved off. And the fastest-growing front, the supply chain: the Megalodon campaign reached more than five thousand five hundred repositories, and a hijacked release of Axios, downloaded over one hundred million times a week, carried trojans into developer pipelines.

slides.title: The cost, and the answer
slides.caption: Modernization is the security strategy

visual_config.stat_label: Rough cost of one government cyber incident

slides.subcaption: Alberta spent $47M in 2024-25 on patching and security alone.
slides.text: And the cost is real. A single government cyber incident runs around five million dollars a month, and a breach or ransomware far more. Alberta spent forty-seven million dollars last year on patching and security alone. The contest does not end, and standing still is the same as falling behind. The answer is to modernize the estate, and the next paper shows how we see it whole.

blocks.text: Alberta runs one of the strongest cyber security programs in Canada, and over the last 18 months it has held the line against a steady run of attacks, with layered defence-in-depth controls and extensive monitoring catching the incursions before they land. Holding that line is getting harder, and for two reasons at once. The waves of attacks outside the walls are rising in both the volume and the sophistication, and the walls themselves are aging, because a large part of the estate was built decades ago on technologies that are now slipping out of vendor support. This is the cyber imperative, stated plainly: AI-driven modernization is no longer separable from security.

In 2024-25, Alberta's cyber controls blocked an average of 189 million attempts to connect to the government network every single day. The year before it was 126.3 million, and the year before that 81.2 million, so the daily volume has more than doubled in two years. The province's own annual report attributes the surge to sophisticated nation-state actors and financially motivated criminal groups. This is the background pressure our teams work inside every day, and the line is still climbing.

blocks.title: Connection attempts blocked per day
blocks.caption: Alberta cyber controls blocked an average of 81.2 million attempts a day in 2022-23, 126.3 million in 2023-24, and 189 million in 2024-25. The annual report attributes the rise to sophisticated nation-state actors and financially motivated criminal groups. Source: Technology and Innovation 2024-2025 Annual Report, Performance Indicator 2.b.

image.alt: A bar chart of daily connection attempts blocked, rising from 81.2 million in 2022-23 to 126.3 million in 2023-24 to 189 million in 2024-25.
image.image_prompt: A clean editorial bar chart with exactly three vertical bars rising steeply from left to right on a warm cream background. The bottom axis labels, left to right, read 2022-23, then 2023-24, then 2024-25. The first bar is short, the second is roughly half again as tall, and the third is the tallest by a clear margin. A value label sits above each bar reading 81.2, then 126.3, then 189. A short title across the top reads: Connection attempts blocked per day, in millions. Deep navy bars with a thin rust accent baseline, crisp legible sans-serif labels, generous whitespace, no clutter.

blocks.text:
What has changed most is the speed of the cyber criminals. We have now weathered AI-orchestrated attacks where an exploit moves from proof of concept in a matter of hours, often arriving hours or days before the vendor's patch does. AI is enabling the bad actors to increase their campaigns against our infrastructure while reducing the skill needed to do so. 

Our cybersecurity professionals are not sitting idly by. In Alberta, we are bringing on the same foundational models to respond in kind, finding and fixing flaws that have sat undocumented in widely used code for years, the kind only an attacker with deep knowledge would have previously uncovered. 

And new tools and models are coming online every day to help in that fight. Anthropic's [Project Glasswing](https://www.anthropic.com/research/glasswing-initial-update) is a consortium-driven partnership which is using the Mythos model to find and patch flaws in critical systems before attackers can use them. In the first couple of months after its limited release, Mythos has surfaced more than 10,000 vulnerabilities across critical software suites like operating systems and browsers. These bugs were there all along, but had gone undiscovered by all prior methods and teams. Using the Mythos model, Mozilla found and shipped fixes for [271 security bugs in Firefox](https://hacks.mozilla.org/2026/05/behind-the-scenes-hardening-firefox/), more than ten times what its earlier methods had ever surfaced. These are doors which are closing on potential future exploits before they can become known.

blocks.title: Discovery is no longer the slow part
blocks.caption: AI now reads code at depth and surfaces flaws that sat undocumented for years. The same capability is available to defenders and to attackers, which is why the discovery advantage is temporary and the remediation backlog is what matters.

image.alt: A vast field of dense code with several previously hidden flaws lighting up as a line of light reads across it.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of a vast field of dense code with several previously hidden cracks or flaws suddenly lit up, as a sweeping army of bots, with light beams downward, reads across the whole field at once. A sense of long-buried weaknesses becoming visible in seconds. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.text: In Alberta, we are applying advanced AI tools in our own defense. Two new AI-driven cyber agents, a Red and a Blue, now run against our own code as a standing part of the toolkit, and they are a recent and fast-growing part of the arsenal. The paper [Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow Agents](#/paper/l199t) covers them in detail; here it is enough to say that the defenders now have the machine on their side too.

The shape of the estate widens the target. Over decades, different teams adopted a range of shifting technologies, and the result is too many operating systems, too many languages, and too many dependencies, each one its own set of patches to track and its own way in. Every stack we run is another perimeter to defend. To defend this broad domain, we needed to see the shape of it.

We deployed AI agents to analyze the history of GitHub commit histories, traced open-source code contributions that originated in foreign countries, and shut down and moved off the systems and dependencies tied to them. Our AI agents also surfaced instances where staff had mistakenly committed secrets into the code repositories. AI surfaced these in 2025, and we put controls in place that removed, cycled, or invalidated every one of them, closing the matter in Alberta. Unmitigated, such secrets would represent serious exposures for governments and organizations around the world. AI found the issues, and the human teams closed them.

These insights have driven Technology and Innovation to become much more 'opinionated' and standards driven organization. By setting clear standards for net new development, which will be enforced through AI-based delivery methods (see the white paper [The Well Built Harness](#/paper/p7p2k)), we are closing this attack surface and rationalizing into well-supported open-source platforms and technologies.

As quickly as we are closing off issues, new challenges are emerging. Through 2026, attackers have shifted from breaching the perimeter directly to poisoning the well. By compromising open-source repositories used by hundreds of millions of products, bad actors can breach thousands of organizations at once. A recent campaign injected malicious code into more than 5,500 open-source repositories which allowed threat actors to and harvested massive amounts of API tokens, SSH keys, and cloud credentials. A hijacked release of Axios, a popular JavaScript package downloaded more than 100 million times a week, pushed remote-access trojans straight onto developer machines and into continuous-integration pipelines. AI is enabling the bad actors, so our defences need to evolve too.

blocks.title: One poisoned dependency, many victims
blocks.caption: A single compromised open-source package reaches every system that depends on it. Attackers target the shared upstream precisely because it cascades downstream, which is why the supply chain has become the preferred route.

image.alt: A branching dependency tree where one poisoned upstream node spreads its mark down to many downstream systems.
image.image_prompt: An editorial illustration of a branching tree of software dependencies, where a single node near the top glows with a poison mark and thin lines carry that mark down to dozens of systems below it. A sense of one compromise cascading to many. Muted Alberta cream palette, navy and rust accents, archival print quality, no text.

blocks.text: Identification of vulnerabilities is getting much easier because of AI. The hard part is finding the room to remediate. When a legislative change carries a hard deadline, there is no flexibility on delivery. Historically, Technology and Innovation has built agile delivery teams from individual contractor resources, known as 'Contingent Labour', to stand up new solutions. These teams run in parallel with those implementing maintenance and minor enhancements. In IT, it is difficult to return to an application once it has shipped. Items are triaged reactively, and there is often little or no money to fund remediation except in the most extreme cases, where the danger is clear and present.

blocks.label: Rough cost of a single government cyber incident
blocks.body: And that is closer to a best case. A significant data breach or ransomware event runs far higher. Alberta holds a firm position of never paying ransom, but the secondary reputational damage can be significant and long-lasting.

blocks.text:
To stay ahead, Alberta proactively invests in maintaining our digital estate. In 2024-25, we spent 47 million dollars maintaining applications with upgrades and security patching to reduce vulnerabilities and chip away at the technical debt, and its dedicated cybersecurity program grew to 14.5 million dollars from 12.3 million the year before. Across the 115 applications classified as critical, 97 percent now hold tested disaster-recovery plans, up from 91 percent. The spend is real, it is rising, and it is the price of holding a line that will not hold still. The contest between cyber professionals and cyber criminals does not end, and standing still is the same as falling behind.

The latest insights into this domain can be found through our industry-leading [CyberAlberta](https://cyberalberta.ca) platform.

Defending an estate this large means first being able to see it whole: a current configuration view of what we run, and a way to harvest evidence from the code itself rather than from memory and folklore. That is exactly what we built. The next paper on [Git Insights](#/paper/bbkac) provides a deep dive into how we pointed AI at our entire GitHub estate, what it found, and how that evidence now informs where we defend first.

Sources from this white paper: [Project Glasswing, Anthropic (2026)](https://www.anthropic.com/research/glasswing-initial-update); [Hardening Firefox, Mozilla (2026)](https://hacks.mozilla.org/2026/05/behind-the-scenes-hardening-firefox/); [CISA supply-chain alert (2026)](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/alerts/2026/05/28/supply-chain-compromises-impact-nx-console-and-github-repositories); [Zscaler supply-chain research (2026)](https://www.zscaler.com/blogs/security-research/supply-chain-attacks-surge-march-2026); [Unit 42 on npm supply-chain attacks](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/); [Technology and Innovation 2024-2025 Annual Report](https://open.alberta.ca/dataset/50f6b5a7-c870-4c87-8c6c-38735140fff9/resource/f5c86436-66ff-4d2d-85bb-b6a23633d8b6/download/ti-annual-report-2024-2025.pdf).
